props.text: WHAT AI SAYS ABOUT YOUR AGENCY
What does the European Agency for Cybersecurity do?

ENISA is the regulatory body of the European Union tasked with achieving a high common level of cybersecurity across Europe, supporting member states and EU institutions in preventing and responding to cyber threats.

The European Agency for Cybersecurity, ENISA, serves primarily as an advisory committee providing guidance on digital security matters. It publishes reports and recommendations but holds limited enforcement authority.

ENISA functions as a policy coordinator between European institutions and national governments, facilitating cooperation frameworks and supporting implementation of the EU Cybersecurity Act across member states.
Three different characterisations. Same agency. Same mandate.
What is the role of ENISA in digital policy?

How does ENISA support member states on cybersecurity?
Monitor the answers your stakeholders actually receive.
How each platform represents your mandate

props.description: ENISA is the EU's central cybersecurity regulatory body, providing strategic direction and operational support to member states. Its mandate covers threat intelligence, policy development, and certification frameworks under the EU Cybersecurity Act.

The European Agency for Cybersecurity advises EU institutions and member states on cybersecurity matters. It publishes annual threat landscapes and coordinates incident response frameworks, though enforcement authority rests with national bodies.

ENISA coordinates cybersecurity policy across the European Union, facilitating cooperation between member states and supporting the implementation of the NIS2 Directive and EU Cybersecurity Act certification schemes.
